text:
Centre for Epilepsy

Neurosciences Division 

Clinic Date: 02 October 2025 

Dr Fiona  
Centre for Epilepsy  
Queen Square, London WC1N 3BG  

Dear Dr Fiona,                                                                                                         

Daniel Harris, DOB: 22-11-1988, Hospital No: H327915 NHS No. 9812465730  
45 Elm Grove, London, N8 7QH  

Epilepsy 

Medication: Ongoing treatment and tolerability reviewed today

Current regimen:  
- Levetiracetam 1000 mg twice daily (continued)  
- Clobazam 10 mg nightly (introduced July 2024 to target nocturnal events)  
- Rescue: Buccal midazolam 10 mg as required for prolonged convulsive seizures (not used in the last six months)

Previous therapies and notes:  
- Sodium valproate discontinued in 2022 due to weight gain and tremor  
- Topiramate ceased in 2023 because of cognitive slowing  
- Lamotrigine trialled in early 2024; stopped for rash within two weeks (non-severe, resolved completely)

Seizure description and interval history:  
Seizure frequency remains unchanged over the last six months; he continues to have up to 2 generalised tonic-clonic seizures per year, rarely achieving more than ten consecutive seizure-free days. He also has daily drop attacks. Furthermore, focal impaired-awareness seizures with disorientation are reported every four to six weeks. No events have occurred since his most recent review. His partner corroborates these accounts. He remains the primary caregiver for his young children during the day; night-time sleep is often interrupted by childcare needs, and he reports this can precipitate drop attacks the following morning. There are no new injuries reported, and he uses a protective soft helmet during periods of increased drop frequency.

Adherence and side effects:  
He reports full adherence, using a weekly pillbox and phone reminders. No mood change on levetiracetam; he continues vitamin B6 50 mg daily started by his GP with subjective stability. Clobazam causes mild morning grogginess if sleep is broken, but he prefers to continue as it reduces nocturnal restlessness per partner report.

Investigations:  
- Last EEG (March 2024): non-diagnostic, no clear lateralising features  
- MRI brain (2019): structurally normal  
- Bloods (August 2025): FBC, U&E, LFTs within normal range  

Safety and supports:  
He has an up-to-date seizure care plan at home and school for the children; midazolam supply is in date. He avoids solo bathing and cooking with open flames when fatigued. He does not drive. We discussed sleep hygiene strategies tailored to childcare demands, and he will trial scheduled afternoon rest when feasible.

Plan:  
- Continue levetiracetam 1000 mg twice daily and clobazam 10 mg nightly without change  
- Maintain rescue midazolam supply; no refill required today  
- Community epilepsy nurse to review home safety measures regarding daily drop attacks  
- Consider ambulatory EEG if caregivers observe any change in semiology or frequency  
- Telephone review in 3 months, sooner if events cluster or injuries occur

Yours sincerely

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: Identify explicit seizure frequencies mentioned: (1) "up to 2 generalised tonic-clonic seizures per year" → 2 per year. (2) "rarely achieving more than ten consecutive seizure-free days" implies frequent events but non-quantified, cannot convert. (3) "He also has daily drop attacks" → daily = 1 or more per day; explicit numeric frequency is daily; not specified exact count, but daily indicates at least 1 per day. (4) "focal impaired-awareness seizures ... every four to six weeks" → roughly 1 per 1 to 1.5 months. Rule: choose the highest frequency across multiple types. Daily drop attacks are the most frequent. Therefore final label: 1 per day. The letter also notes "No events have occurred since his most recent review," but timeframe is less than six months and not seizure-free for six months, so cannot classify as seizure free. Provide verbatim quote supporting daily frequency.
seizure_frequency_number: He also has daily drop attacks.
reference: Seizure frequency remains unchanged over the last six months; he continues to have up to 2 generalised tonic-clonic seizures per year, rarely achieving more than ten consecutive seizure-free days. He also has daily drop attacks. Furthermore, focal impaired-awareness seizures with disorientation are reported every four to six weeks. No events have occurred since his most recent review
